**[5:21](https://youtu.be/sxqfoa2ZnQE?t=321)** — our spending authority which again is more of our credit limit uh often lags at least one fiscal year behind our cash flow. We spent that cash in fiscal year 25. We get the authority granted back in the next fiscal year that's put back on fiscal year 25, but we don't actually have the ability to levy to recover those funds until fiscal year 27. **[9:44](https://youtu.be/sxqfoa2ZnQE?t=584)** — [clears throat] >> So, moving off of our cash uh balances, let's talk about unspent authorized budget. So, it's spending authority um that the state grants us. So, authority is the ceiling um and it's permission to spend, not necessarily cash. So, um, every year we get a maximum authorized budget. Unfortunately, it's set after our fiscal year ends due to some variables that we run through, uh, with our state reporting. But, um, that's again, think of that as a credit limit or the max we can spend, not necessarily the cash we're getting in or the full number full amount of expenses we have. And that's driven primarily by our regular program district cost. So that's the state cost per pupil that's

**[10:29](https://youtu.be/sxqfoa2ZnQE?t=629)** — adjusted by SSA. Again, that's only a portion of our total receipts uh that we get across the district. But that's when people talk about SSA and how does that affect all this? That's the regular program district cost, the cost per pupil, which again is a portion of our total receipts, but not all of our receipts. Um and then we have additional levies that such as our cash reserve levy, um our state aid that we've talked about. And then the other allowed adjustments are modified supplemental amounts. um those of you who have been on the board uh for a while. We do this usually in the fall and a little bit in the spring. Um but those are those things that we um request through the SPRC to get additional funding authority. So again, for that special ed deficit, we're allowed to request that authority back. Um it's what's called a class action uh through the SPRC. They

**[11:15](https://youtu.be/sxqfoa2ZnQE?t=675)** — take all school districts at once, approve them all at once. Um the one-off items uh that the SBRC approves are uh more rare. Uh but that's uh one that all districts submit on. And so that modified supplemental amount again just grants you the authority but doesn't necessarily give you [snorts] a cash revenue source for that um request. >> So it the MSA revenue is paid after we incur after we paid the cost. >> Correct. Yep. And about a whole fiscal year later to be honest. >> Okay. So just a very basic and rough equation to give you for an unspent authorized budget. So you take your authorized budget, whatever your max according to the state level is, and you subtract that from your actual expenditures to

**[12:00](https://youtu.be/sxqfoa2ZnQE?t=720)** — date. And those actual expenditures would be the cash you have outstanding for any expenditures. So again, a real quick simple example, um, if district's authorized budget is 10 million and they spend 9.2 on the year, then their unspent authorized budget is 800,000. So that 800,000 would carry forward in future years. It doesn't expire unless you use it and then it can be used when when spending authority is tight if you have the cash. So it gives you that flexibility. You know, maybe should SSA not be at a rate that the district feels comfortable in in working in the future with if you have that authority and you have the cash, you have that ability to offset that. So what uh unspent authorized budget is not it is not cash. So when you see

**[12:46](https://youtu.be/sxqfoa2ZnQE?t=766)** — those reports um you know that we have 3 million in unspent authorized budget or 5 million that's not necessarily a a cash level that we have um it's not fund balance um it's again more of a credit uh limit than anything not savings in a bank account and not automatically spendable without money. So um if you have the cash you can do it if you don't have the cash you can't. So again you still need the actual resources such as cash fund balances and revenues to spend it.
